speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph Carver in London, England. Joseph, you were saying you just volunteered for the-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: For the animal clinic. Yes, I came to the animal clinic. And I had to report to two veterinary surgeons. When they told me-- because other prisoners worked there. When they told me to go inside and report to the veterinary surgeons, I was surprised to see two Jewish guys, veterinary surgeons, Hungarian Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was completely surprised because as far as I remember, veterinary surgeons always were Gentiles-- Poles, of course, or Germans, maybe Hungarians, because I don't know very much about the Hungarians. But I was completely taken aback. And they said, yes, they were veterinary surgeons. Now among themselves, they spoke Hungarian. I picked it up. I picked all these languages up-- Russian, Hungarian, Slovakian, even Greek because we had some Greek Jews and Russian people, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these two guys were discussing that, now that they have me, and I'm speaking German, whether I haven't been there planted. They were afraid because they were trying to think how they could-- could cook the broth. Because they killed an animal, a dead animal, which they, of course, have to certify. And the carcass had to be turned over to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before they turn over the carcass, they had to open up the horse inside. We had horses and cows there. And they helped themselves to a little bit of the offal there. So they were cooking. And I said to them-- I was speaking Yiddish to them because all of these Jews speak Yiddish. And I was telling them, I said, look, brothers, I'm your brother. I'm not a plant. I speak German because I was born in Germany. I was brought up with that language. And here everyone speaks German. But I'm your brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, they said, a horse died. We have to certify it. We are waiting for the brigadier to come in. And this will be taken over to the SS. And they will eat it. So, he says, while they are feasting, we should have a little feast of ourselves. So they gave me a little bit of their broth as well. Then we had cows. And I said to one of them, cows give milk. Can't we have a bit of milk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, he says. They've all got tuberculosis. He says, we don't want to have that. He says, only fools, people who want to die, they will drink the milk. We won't touch their milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked there till the end of the liquidation of the Auschwitz concentration camp. And it came about that, in January, before the Russian advance, the brigadier called us all up. And he said to me, you will be in charge of the wagon with all the medicines, all the medical staff, like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] syringes and injections and all different kinds what they give to the animals. And there will be a guard on top of the wagon. So we always traveled at night time. I took that wagon, which was loaded up with all the medicaments and all the instruments. And the German guard, who was a Yugoslavian fellow, young fellow, fairly cocky devil, always tried to put his bayonet onto my neck, you know, like poking me that he is there, which game was irritating. And it was hurting. So out we came of Auschwitz, of the concentration camp. The sky was alight, just like daylight. They put these flamethrowers through. It lit the whole sky up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Poland, the highway is very narrow, just enough for one vehicle to go that way. If another vehicle got to go the other way, then they got the ditches. Somebody got to end up into ditches.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these Polish horses, they're not used to the traffic. And what, with all this noise at night [? the tanks ?] and the big, heavy lorries, they went bedlam. And my horse bolted, just bolted up in the air.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole wagon went into the ditch with the SS guard. The wagon actually fell on him. I was lucky. I jumped on the side. When I saw what happened, I got hold of a lorry, went highway with the lorry-- looked back, I could see other lorry behind me. I said, oh god, they are going to shoot me in the back because they can see my stripes. I tried to camouflage myself, go underneath till after a little while, I saw other prisoners marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I jumped off from that lorry and marched to the other side where the prisoners were. And I said, where are you marching? He said, nobody knows. We are marching. So we went on for a few days. Then we got cattle wagons coming for us. And we finished up in Gross-Rosen Concentration Camp. The Polesmen, they saw us. They said, oh, the Jews are coming, the stinking Jews. Get rid of the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they could see our yellow star, so they knew straightaway we are Jews. And the 'P' means Polish. So they had it there. Now all these Poles-- most of them, anyway-- they were criminals. And they were running the camp. So they got hold of the Jews. They beat them to death. Some of them, poor, old Hungarian Jews. They couldn't fight back. They didn't understand Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to one of the German officers, this Poles insulted the Fuhrer. Oh, he said, yes, what did he say? Oh, I said, a very, very dirty word. He said, is it this? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to him, and he shot him. He took his revolver out and shot him. And this was the biggest rascal, the biggest murderers among these. When the other Poles saw that, that he was killed thus, they just went away. They all got frightened. So we Jews kept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans came over to us. And he said, you are going to Buchenwald. So we said, where is Buchenwald? Oh, it's-- it's in Germany, in Thuringia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now from geography, I knew that Thuringia was Weimar. And if you go to Weimar, that Buchenwald couldn't be very far away. Because it's all forest. And the name 'Wald' means forest. So it must be nearby there. Anyway, we arrived in Buchenwald late in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we arrived by these cattle wagons. We arrived there by these cattle wagons. And in Buchenwald, also, you see, we were given different numbers than we had in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like actually arriving there? What did you see on arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very big concentration camp, mainly for political prisoners. It had-- the majority of people there were Germans, for political crimes. They had also some German Jews who were there before the war and just after the war. And they had-- and the people were coming, arriving up from Auschwitz. And they had some who came there before from different part of the Ukraine where they had concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So straight away, every morning, Germans came in. Everybody up, everybody up. And we had to march out. First we had to get to washed-- ice-cold water. Then we were given coffee, some sort of coffee. And those who had something to eat, ate. If you had nothing to eat, you didn't eat. And we were marching out. So I was in a commando, which went to do bomb disposals. Like, they bombed a place. And we had to go and look what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: If any people were still underneath, we had to dug them out. And if there was anything else, like, we had to take bricks-- put bricks in-- on a special place, brick to brick, and iron to iron, and metal to metal. And if we found any clothes, you know, or if we found any bodies, we had to take them out. And then somebody would come, certify. And this went on and on. Now, we were already told, by one of the friendly guards-- well, I say friendly guards, well, among the criminals, there was maybe somebody was a little bit more friendly than the others. And he said, boys, no pilfering. No thieving. If you go back to camp, and if we find anything, you will be shot. You will be a dead chicken. So I'm warning you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those who believed in fate wouldn't go back with a carrot or a turnip or a potato because it was theft. They would be shot. Anyway, I never-- never dreamt of ever taking anything back to camp. If I found anything, I ate it straightaway, raw.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't ask about cooking a potato. If you found a potato, you ate it raw. You didn't hide it. You didn't look for any cooking facilities, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we stayed at Auschwitz. And every day was something different. People were hanged-- Is that Auschwitz, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald. People were hanged. We had to witness the hanging. In my block, two Ukrainians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] committed cannibalism. One of their friends died. They opened him up. They took out his liver and his heart. And they were eating it. And in the morning, when the criminal police came, these people were hanged because they were cannibals. They committed cannibalism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that, Joseph? Or did you hear of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it-- it happened at night. We saw it in the morning. We saw the dead bodies. And we saw the two culprits. But at night, we didn't see what was happening because it had to be quiet. Nobody had to make a noise. Everybody was afraid. And there was a long day ahead. And there were so many killings, so many hangings. Every day was something different.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, they calling out, all Jews out, all Jews out. So that's funny. We are here with all the different nationalities. Why Jews? Something must be brewing. So I said to one of the German couples, why only the Jews? Oh, he says, they only want Jews. He didn't say German because he was a German. He said 'they,' meaning the SS. They only want Jews. So we came out. And I was thinking, should I go with the Germans? Should I take off my star? Should I go? Should I not go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked somebody else. And he said, look at this mountain. There was a mountain where people who couldn't work anymore were left dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: A human mountain?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they joined the mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: A human mountain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Human mountain, yes. One on top of the other was laying, either to be incinerated or to be shot or to be burned alive. And he said to me, well, he says, you can finish up. The best thing is to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I said, I've got to take [? potluck. ?] And I was standing there, and we were marching. We had to march from the camp outside Buchenwald. Through the back of the town-- as we were walking through the town, the population was very hostile. They saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They stoned us. They spat at us. They called us abusive names. They wanted to kill us, but the SS kept them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans took us through a different route. And we marched. And some people fell. And they were shot by the guards who were behind me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also fell. And a friend of mine lifted me up, and he pushed me. He says, they will kill you. They will shoot you in the back. Walk, walk, walk. And I walked for a little while, then I fell down again. Then the fellow who lifted me up, he fell down. And I lifted him up. So we helped each other. We tried. You know, we tried. And this went on and on, endless, no food, no nothing. Then all of a sudden, they said, hold. We stopped. And they were talking to each other. They were-- were waiting for an order. And they didn't have the proper order to get on. Eventually the order came. And they said, everybody aboard. The cattle wagons arrived. And the destinations were given. We were never told where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting in this cattle section, and we went from Thuringia into Saxony. And I could read the names of the little railway station. And I knew we were not far from Leipzig. Eventually, we finished up at the railway station in Leipzig. The train needed water for the locomotive. So I had like, a little tin in front of me. Everybody had like a tin. A tin was there to put food in it or whatever, or drink water. So I jumped down from the train to get a little bit of water from the pump.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I jumped down trying to fill it, somebody poked a gun to my nose and said, you saboteur, you dog, you criminal. It was a young boy of 14 with a SS uniform from the Hitler Youth. They have a special uniform-- but black, instead of brown, black because they were a unit assigned to the SS. And then there was a old fellow who saw that. Oh, he must've been in his 60s, I should imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. He says, leave him to me. I will deal with this. And he came to me. And I said, I'm only a student. I said, I was born in this town. Rubbish, he says, you are all saboteurs. You are all criminals. And he emptied the water for me. And he gave me a beating. My face was swollen. My head was swollen. And lucky, the SS officer came to my rescue. And he says, oh, you. You leave him alone. He says, we're going now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to go back onto the wagon minus water, minus my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tin. Because a tin is a lifeline. Without a tin, how can you eat food, even if you find anything? So I had to go up. And we were sitting on the floor. And there was about 80 of us sitting there-- or standing. And slowly the train left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it left Saxony, and we were already on Czechoslovakian soil because the names were different. And we went through, like, Carlsbad, Marienbad, Pilsen. In Pilsen we stayed. It was the beer city. We could smell the hops. We could smell the beer but we couldn't drink it. [LAUGHS] And we stayed. And lucky for us, there was alarm because the Americans started bombing the area. So we heard the sirens. The Germans made themselves scarce. And we were told to hide under the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we saw some of our people running into the forest. So I thought, well, they were running. Maybe there's something-- so I ran into the forest, too. And everybody on his own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then somebody said, oh, they've got dogs coming. So I climbed on the tree, right up in the tree on the top. And I hid myself in the branches. I looked down. I could see everything. And people were looking up. They couldn't see me. But I didn't know that. I took it for granted that maybe they couldn't see me because it's so dense.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two guards came with dogs. And the dog barked in front of the tree. And he said to the other Germans, somebody's up there. The dog knows.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he says, no. He says, yes, he says, he knows. So he took out his machine gun. And he started shooting at the branches. And slowly, slowly, the branches were falling down. And I-- I could see all this. And I said, well, the next bullet I will drop down. And I remember, when we used to go to the cinema-- although we were not allowed to go to cinemas, but clandestinely we went through because they didn't know whether we were Jewish or not. A little boy, you see, has got a ticket and goes in the cinema. And we used to see the jungle films, and

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought to myself, I'm going to be just like the monkeys. And I'm going to drop dead because the shooting. So the siren again went. And they ran for cover with the dogs. So once I saw them disappear, I was looking out and I could see another-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] another-- a lot of people marching. They also had the striped clothing. And I slowly came down from the tree and ducked like a rabbit and went sort of this way, like zigzag, you know, till I reached the other people. And I said to them, where are you going? They said, we are going straight. We don't know. Where you coming-- oh, I just said, Pilsen. I said, I come from that. Oh, they said, they are on the other side. So apparently for Buchenwald, there were many wagons of these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we marched. And we came to a fortress, a fortress town, ditches on both sides. We marched into the fortress, down where in the olden days, they must have had this specially for horses. And we just laid down on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody said, there are no German guards. What happened? Nobody wanted to know. We were there, we were tired from marching all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the night, we heard a lot of shooting. Then somebody said, go and see what is happening. Maybe we will be shot. Maybe they're going to shoot us now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I went out, I could hear Russian. And then, all of a sudden, somebody said to me in Russian, stop! So they took me to the commanding officer, who was sitting on top of the tank.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked at his face. I saw he was a Russian Jewish officer. I spoke to him in Yiddish. And he could see my yellow star. So he knew that I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said to me, recite the Shema for me. I recited for him. He said, what else do you know? So whatever I remembered, what I learned in Hebrew school, I told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yes. Now I know you are a Jew. So he gave me tea, a piece of bread. He said, be careful. Don't eat too much because if you overeat yourself, you will be dead. I said, there are some of my friends inside, you know, down there below. So he gave orders for the Russian nurses to go there with tea and with bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I said, where are we? Where is this place? What's the name of this place? He says, Theresienstadt. Didn't mean a thing to me. So they told me that this was a concentration camp where mainly Austrian, German, and Czechoslovakian Jews were held. It was a model camp. But a lot of them went to Auschwitz. And this is the remnant, sort of. And the war is finished. They are our liberation army. So we were liberated on the 8th of May. What did you think at that moment in time, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jubilation, freedom, and relief, great relief, knowing that you haven't got any more of these cursed German guards with the guns, with the bayonets, breathing down your neck. Everything would be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in this place, I caught typhoid and dysentery. And I was sent to one of the military hospitals. I stayed there for about a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after the month, I was convalescing out for a few weeks. During that time, I thought I would go back to Poland to see if anybody is alive. I went back. I went to the magistrate as I came from the railway station. I said, I came to inquire if anybody from my family is registered. And the magistrate was in session.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to me, you dirty Jew, are you still alive? If you don't clear out in a few minutes, in five minutes, you will get shot. So I heard this. I didn't want it to go any further.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went straight back to the station and back to Czechoslovakia. When I came back, of course, in these two or three days, lots of things happened. It's like, every day new things happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't go anymore into the town, due to the epidemic. And I had to go to the next town. And there, they told me-- because I was still in this military hospital in a sort of annex.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, there's a commission here from England, taking children to England. But they've got to be 14 years old. I think I was 17 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of my friends says, well, I'm your brother. And I know you're 14. So he called one of the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] officers, like, repatriation officers. And he says, that's my brother here. I want him to go to England. He's 14. So he wrote out-- it's the repatriation pass, which I actually have with me. And on it says 1/7/1931. So the difference in my age was given as three years younger instead of my real age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this went on, oh, right up till my retirement, and before I got my pension. And then, of course, I was told by the Holocaust Survivors Center here to go and get my proper certificate from Germany so that I get my pension in time of my real age. So this is another thing [LAUGHS] that went off my chest. And I am free to breathe. And we came to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed in Windermere, in the Lake District. We-- we were there for three months convalescing. It was the real-- the best time of our life there. We had a superb time. We had-- each one of us had a bedroom to themselves, new clothing, new underwear. We were given pocket money. We had plenty of food. We went on outings. We went to see films. We played games. We had cultural activities. And then, after three months, we were selected to go to various hostels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you, who selected you, or who was responsible for bringing you over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Jewish relief unit, which was the Jewish-- what do they call this? [? Open ?] House. Yes, this was also the CBS, Central British Fund. Only at that time, they had a different name. Yes, it were the Jewish refugee committees who selected us. And we came to London. And from there, everybody made their own way. As I had some family here, some distant cousins of my father, I stayed with them. And so eventually, my life started to get a little bit normal. And then I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I started work as a dental technician. And then I gave that up. I went to the London School of Economics. I went there for a few weeks. Harold Laski, he was at the time the principal. He took me on. But it was far too much, too advanced. I couldn't keep up with the studies. I left that. And I was doing different jobs because as a alien, you had to register with the police. And then I thought I would go and study. And I studied in the evening college to improve my English and general knowledge.
